36. Audience Targeting Best Practices 4-5-25.mp4 Thank you. All right, sweet. What's going on, everybody? Welcome in. Weekly Inner Circle group call. Today, we're going through some targeting best practices. I'll begin this question. Uh, rather frequently, and I realized they don't necessarily have like a direct training on it. So I figured that we would spend the time together here to. go through some of the thinking behind. how we go about targeting on the different platforms, cold audiences, warm audiences. Um, also how we're kind of thinking with. pairing what and who we target with the creatives that we're going to run towards those individuals. So kind of be like a mix of. written lessons, and then like literally going into the ad managers and just building out some target audiences. So first thing I want to do is just. explain some best practices. And after explaining those best practices, like I said, we'll jump into the ad accounts themselves. and we'll go through some targeting. Without further ado, let's jump into it. Okay, so for starters, When we talk. in general about targeting. There's obviously two main buckets. of targeting types that we can. you know, drill into. which is cold. and warm. A lot of people nowadays, they... Heavily biased towards warm, from what I've noticed, if they have the luxury of doing so. Not everybody has warm audiences. A lot of people obviously just are limited to cold. Some people consider warm to just be retargeting. I do not. So again, we'll go through kind of like what each one of these means. So first of all, Corn in general. Essentially means, you know, people. We do not know you. Okay, and there's varying degrees of cold audiences. We've talked at length about this concept that comes from the book, The Ultimate Selling Machine by Chet Holmes. He calls it stadium selling. And in the book, stadium selling. He essentially says, like, you're down here and you're pitching to a demographic of people. He likes to say there's about 3% to 4% of people that are considered in market. Even layers out. To the needs-convinced demographics. Needs convinced. Is another big favorite of mine when it comes to cold audiences, but generally not something that you need to target initially. And then you have these other two. This is mass market. Mass market are people that you have to bring into awareness about what you have, convince them on why they need it. A lot less cost effective. And then that final 33%. This is just non-buyers. These people are typically deemed as impossible to convert in the eyes of this uh this author chet holmes and I tend to agree with that. So to be clear, the differences between these are really important to understand. And we can also target these people with not only what we can select, but also the messaging that we pair with these different things. So one of the things that I want to make really clear, when we go into the ads managers, and we build out some of these audiences. One of the most common questions I get about targeting is, is there a way to literally just target in market audiences? Right. And give or take the platform, the answer is yes. If I'm using Google AdWords as an example, I didn't. I have ways that I can absolutely target in-market audiences. Because I can search based on keywords and search terms. And I can essentially create audience types based on intent. Okay. When I'm using Facebook and Instagram as an example, there tends to be a much heavier bias. On that platform, towards broad targeting in general and creating larger audience types and trusting the platform's prediction machine that they've built. To be able to have them identify. Who's most probable to convert for you? Without you having necessarily. Be smart enough or be capable enough to create different audience segments that are more probable and less probable to convert. So Google AdWords has that capability. Facebook and Instagram do not. TikTok also, I'd say, has the capability to define in-market demographics. Because we can go beyond just demographic traits. behavior traits and interests we also can target things like hashtags and you know, different video types that people are watching. The other cool thing about Google AdWords and YouTube in general is that we get the opportunity to target people based on channels and individual videos. Which is cool because again. That gives us the opportunity to go after this in-market demographic. With a lot higher you know, probability that we're actually getting in front of those people. Okay. Again, just to be clear, kind of summarize what I just said. Facebook and Instagram, heavily biased towards broad. You got to trust the prediction machine a lot more on Facebook and Instagram. There's not as much of a need to actually try to target in-market demographics. It's more about the messaging on that platform. Ah. To be clear, Again, Google AdWords, TikTok, they do give us the ability to target in-market demographics. Now, what's the difference between these? So in-market means they are people who are actively looking for solutions that your company offers. They want to buy what you have. They know they have the problem or they just know they need the solution that you offer. And they're aware of it. And they're typically actively seeking something to help them get that result or outcome or solve whatever problem they have. That's all in-market demographic means. Actively searching. Buying solutions. Okay. They want to buy something. They need what you have. They need to be convinced. These people are generally aware. That they have the problem. Okay. They're aware. That they could do something to solve it, but they're not necessarily actively seeking a solution. So therefore, that demographic is generally defined to be again people who don't know you. They're aware they have the problem, but they're not actively seeking a solution. Okay, that demographic is typically a little less. Little less ROAS. Still a great audience to target, especially at scale. However, our messaging typically has to change to exactly as described. We have to be more persuasive. We have to. You know, kind of create the urgency that they lack to solve that problem. Mask market is a whole different animal. Mass market is not for the weak. mass market requires you to learn how to convert. non-buyers who like don't even necessarily identify that they have the problem that you can solve as a company. and convince them that they do. So an example of this, one of my favorite ones is. on that Dan Locke deal back in the day, you know, we got to the point where we were convincing like Uber drivers. and nurses. like i remember a lot of the testimonials became like Oh, I was going to college and I quit to become a high ticket closer, you know? These are people that, you know, had a totally different career path that I was able to successfully convince. And convert into high ticket closers, you know, and abandon their profession. These would be strategies that we've created, like the harvester, as an example. That's perfect for like mass market. So anyway, in terms of profitability. What you always want to remember is your highest profitability comes from. the in-market demographics when you're targeting cold audiences. Needs convinced demographics, typically less profitable, still profitable, but less profitable. Mass market, less profitable than the first two. Um, again, totally fine if you get to the point where you're mass market, but you generally want to move in waves through those three layers first. You'll get to give or take the audience type that you're targeting. You can get... a low couple hundred thousand in spend and in market. For some of you who have like really, really small audiences, like maybe like under 100,000 total addressable market people in your audience. Obviously your in-market demographic is literally going to be like low thousands. Your needs-convinced demographic might be like low tens of thousands and your mass market might be considered like. You know, 50,000 to 70,000 of those total people in your audience. If you're targeting, like, real estate or stocks or... sales, you know, something that's huge as an industry or niche. Um, some product that has, like, everybody as an example, needs a house. Almost everybody has cars. These are like mass, mass industries and niches. That obviously would have far larger in-market needs convinced in the mass market demographics. So logically, in those, I would have the capacity to spend literally hundreds of thousands of dollars a month. Just targeting in-market messaging and audience types. Before I'd have to rotate out of it to target demographics and then eventually be on that mass market. I've had clients that, without exaggeration, can spend $ 500 to $700,000 a month. And they're technically still within like a majority of that going to end market and then a minority going to needs-convinced, you know? So. Keep this in mind. This is one of those fundamental lessons of court audience targeting that you want to be actively thinking with. Especially factoring in the platform logic that I just articulated. Okay, very important to consider. Okay, now warm audiences, most people. Would simply consider these like retargeting audiences. And although, yes, you know, that's absolutely true. Um, you know that's technically like a very small portion of it—uh, most of this, for what I consider warm, is like website audiences for 180 days. Um, any kind of lead form interaction, either submitted or non-submitted. You can typically do up to 90 days on the different platforms for that remarketing audience. You obviously have the social page. This could be, as an example, like... the Facebook page, the Instagram page. Uh, the YouTube page, the Tick tock account. And all of these, you typically have upwards of like four to five different audience types within them that you can target. These are also generally all available for a 365-day windup. These are like people who visited the profile, interacted with the page, interacted with posts, message the page, save post, follow the page, like the page, etc. Subscribers, playlist viewers, things like that. 365-day audiences here are really good. They... are typically very large. and filled with people who are probable to convert. And if you notice, like, obviously they're not all targeting just followers. which is a really important consideration there. In comparison, in most instances, they're actually targeting people who've just had interaction with the account or some content or some ads in some instances. Again, typically considered broad, and then the broadest audience that can be considered the coldest out of all of your warm audiences that you could target. Are video viewers and for video viewers, we have as low as like three seconds, which I generally never target. 10 seconds, 15 seconds. And then we go up the thresholds where we have like 25%, 50%, 75%, and then 95%, followed by the final 100%, give or take the platform. These are all the video view thresholds that you get the opportunity to target. So these are all really good. I'm definitely a big fan. Um. Creating every audience type here that I just articulated. And. Going into the audience section specifically and just making these and then going after them on. Especially on accounts that have high spend. Because you accumulate obviously a lot of people inside of these warm audiences as your spend continues to increase. And then in addition to that, large organic presence. Easy layup deals typically come from warm audiences. Now, the upside of cold, just to be clear, before we continue into just building out some audience types as examples. Are cold is generally far more scalable. Cold is where you really get off a lot of the spend. You have a much higher probability to simply put, scale. deal with less fatigue. Get tuned. Do certain conversion mechanisms at a higher frequency. I'll give you a great example of this. We have clients that can only do one webinar a month. because they mainly target their warm audiences. And a lot of their strategies revolve around just like using content to fill up warm audiences. They have a large organic presence and then they just kind of, you know, get stuck, give or take the skill of their sales teams. To targeting people who are already aware of them, which is again just limiting them to warm audiences, the size of those warm audiences, and the ability to continue filling those warm audiences, directly impacts the scale potential of how much spend I can get off. Just targeting warm audiences. Comparatively, we have a client right now, as an example, they do twice a week live webinars. And a hundred percent of what they've done up to this point has just been cold targeting. They're also in a very large demographic of the real estate niche. So talk about scale potential. I mean, there's generally in that case, almost to a degree, like no cap, you know, on what they could spend. On those cold audience types and because they're cold, because they're just continuously reaching people who have no idea who they are. They get the benefit of doing those twice a week webinars. As a result of that, you know, again, warm audience types, once a month webinars. So one main revenue driver, cold audience types, twice a week webinars. You see what I mean? Just a lot more scale potential there. Also, you know, contrary to most advertiser opinions, you can actually generally experience lower costs. Cost per result, to be clear, is what I'm emphasizing here. From cold audience types. Because again, they're just larger. So there's generally more people that are deemed probable to convert within them. Also, before we just go and start building some audience types, I just want to reiterate this key lesson, which is an audience's size dictates the scale potential. So some audiences, like let me give you two examples here. Let's say that one audience has a million people. And you could consider this warm or cold in this example, it doesn't necessarily matter. And then let's say that in example number two, we have 100 million people. That are targetable in comparison. Keep in mind, when we talk about standard events. OK, standard events, meaning doesn't matter what platform you're on. What you're optimizing that campaign for as an outcome dictates how many people out of everybody that you're targeting that you're actually probable to reach. All the ad platforms do is just bias towards people that are probable to convert. They want to prioritize user experience on the platforms, so they try to avoid showing people things that they're not probable to actually convert on. As much as users generally like to articulate that they hate ads, what they hate worse is being targeted by stuff that just has no relevance to them. Does anybody remember when Elon bought Twitter? Of what kind of ads were serving on Twitter initially? You guys remember that? There was, you know, prior to that point, huge advertisers that were on the platform. You know, Disney, Apple. They come as top of mind examples for people that pulled out. And just stopped advertising for a little bit there. They stopped virtue signaling, and they do advertise there again, but regardless. The point I'm trying to make is as soon as like all the big advertisers pulled out. There was like really silly ads that started to target people. And it actually became like a pretty big joke. There would be ads for like random toys that were targeting, you know, 50 to 60 year old. Hedge fund owners who don't even have kids, you know, there'd be like, Just total bullshit products, you know, that have no relevance to your life and like. You just see them and you're kind of like, what the fuck is this, you know? And people never really noticed until, like I said, like all the big advertisers pulled out and there was no longer like contextual ads that were built for them. So, okay, what I'm trying to make is users actually appreciate having things that are specific to like what they actually want, you know? Put in the chat if you agree with that logic, like you'd rather see ads that are built for you rather than just seeing random ads. Right. So, okay, point being. When we have a standard event, and that we optimize a campaign for. That dictates who we're going to get in front of. Okay. That's the moral of the story. When we target people. Who? Let's say we're optimizing around like scheduled calls. Or let's say we're optimizing around purchases or submitting an application or some custom conversion of some kind. The point is, is these... standard events that we select. They dictate. You know, who we're actually probable to reach out of everybody that we have. Selected in our audience type, again, doesn't matter whether it's warm or cold. All that matters is the standard event that we have selected and the size of the audience. Okay. So if I have a million-person audience, and again, I optimize around whatever the standard event is. I might generally reach, like, 10 to upwards of 30 percent of the entire audience that's available for me to target, just based on the standard event that I select. Okay. This is a very important thing to understand. Because when you do cold or warm advertising. You want to have, in general, a larger audience available for you to target because that widens the amount of people available for you to target. With whatever you're optimizing your campaign for. The larger the audience size, the more people there are for you to target. The more people there are for you to target, the more longevity that campaign has before it fatigues. And before your frequency just drives through the roof. The longer a campaign has before it fatigues, the more you can scale into a campaign. Without running into as many problems. Okay. Problems meaning instability, fluctuations, fatigue, random spikes in cost. The larger the audience is that you have to reach with whatever you're optimizing for, the more stability your campaign generally has. Whether it's warm or cold, that's a very key lesson to factor in. Okay, so real quick. Just before we continue, I'd like to jump into the actual accounts now. And just build some audiences with you guys. But I want to make sure I just answer any questions on that logic before we continue. Does anybody have any questions? Feel free to unmute yourself and ask away if you do. I got one, Jeremy. If you're going to. Um, I hope this rumor that I don't know Facebook is getting more and more better to if you even do like cold campaigns they even like try to get the warm people do you have any experience on that. Yeah, that happens in almost every campaign now. Again, they're going to go after the people that are most probable to convert. And on Facebook, especially. Most accounts now are defaulted to what they call advantage detailed targeting or just advantage audiences, give or take what your account is labeled as. Sometimes they have both. That generally just means it's going to try to reach anybody that's probable to convert beyond who you've targeted. So the way that you would work around that is through exclusions. If you wanted to target true cold audiences. On a platform like Facebook or Instagram, you would actually exclude your warm audiences. Keep in mind, though, if your account is defaulted to... The advantage detailed targeting or that little checkbox has gone away that says 'reach people beyond this audience type that you've selected.' Then you technically are just giving the targeting suggestions. You're not actually definitively saying, 'go after these people and ignore these people.' It technically can still go after the people that it deems probable to convert. Yeah, we talked about this inside of the Master Internet Marketing class in week three. I believe starting in the third and fourth cohort, we talked about these lessons. Where you don't even necessarily need to set up retargeting campaigns nowadays. On Facebook and Instagram, especially because. The platform and the campaigns, even if they're totally cold audiences, just does the retargeting for you now. Got it. That's how it goes. So yeah, it's definitely not a myth. That's very true. Cool. Other questions? Samar, you're unmuted. Do you have anything? Oops, sorry, man. That was an accident. I'm good. It's no problem. It's all good, bro. Ash, you got something? Yeah, man, hey, man. So basically, you know, like I totally, I'm really starting to understand this theory now after you know, many months of being in the group. It's like we've got this one probiotics client. We knew the website was bad. We listened to what you said, which was that you said the hooks were the main problem. We started creating lots of different hooks. We even went for the most mass-market. Hooks to this 35 million dollar audience who'd be interested in probiotics and yes, the performance did improve slightly on the same landing, but like, the ad fatigue so quick it's like— because I haven't had the experience— I still don't understand how you're able to get so much longevity out of your ads because I haven't experienced it. You know, I don't I don't ever necessarily brag about experiencing like longevity out of campaigns. You know, I fall victim to the same things that you guys do with having to overcome ad fatigue. And give or take the audience size and the hooks width. That all determines fatigue. Inflationary events that are outside of our control can also increase our fatigue cycles to be faster. Here's the thing. When I say like having wide hooks, You've heard me talk about this lesson of majority hooks, okay? Majority hooks mean that out of everybody you're targeting in your current audience, you have found the hook, okay, or the hooks. That have the most appeal to that target audience, the widest quantity of people out of those you're targeting. Identify as that's a hook they're going to be responsive to. When you have a minority hook. Okay. It generally is a very thin hook. That a small portion of your target audience are going to be responsive to. Okay, when you have a small minority hook. That means that it appeals to a tiny demographic out of everybody you're targeting. And it also means that you're going to naturally have a far faster fatigue cycle. OK, so you can experience more longevity in a campaign when you find the majority hook. That appeals most to that audience. You're going to have a lot faster fatigue when you have a minority hook. You're going to have a lot faster fatigue. When there's just inflationary events. Inflationary events meaning like... February is a great example of this. I think every advertiser here can relate to February. Super Bowl. Valentine's Day weekend. Followed by President's Day. You could have launched ads earlier in that week. And it would have fatigued by that weekend. and then relaunching your campaign. Even if it had only been a week, you would have gotten better performance out of the relaunch with some fresh creatives following that inflationary event. Sometimes. You can let it ride. and your costs will drop. But in most instances, when there's some kind of inflationary event that can rapidly like increase spend. It's better to just kind of reset the model and treat it like it's advertising to get better results anyway. And then the, uh... The final thing there to kind of comment on is. Look, at the end of the day, spend also dictates. fatigue cycles. You know, like I've had campaigns where we're spending, like, $30,000 to $50,000 a day. And. I won't be able to ride those out for more than like a week, maybe less. So the amount of spend that we get off also dictates how fast something's going to fatigue. And then last thing, audience size. So if there's a very small audience size, Even if you even if you target 100 million people, keep in mind. That doesn't necessarily matter. Audience size is really deemed by the platform for who's probable to convert. Based on your standard event and all your messaging. So if you have, like I said, if you tie all that together, if you have a small minority hook. Even if you have a big audience or a small audience, if you're optimizing around a standard event, with a small minority hook, that's also going to cause rapid fatigue because you're just not going to get in front of a lot of people. Statistically, from like a data perspective, you can generally tell if that's happening. Let's say I target 100 million people. And I only reach like 20,000, and I start to see my frequency climb. That's a direct data-driven example of what I'm mentioning there. You're seeing fatigue happen in real time. Because you're watching a small, tiny audience get hit with your ads. And then you're seeing the frequency drive up on those ads. Typically, you can tell you're going to have some longevity to a campaign. When you get a lot of reach, and you don't necessarily see the frequency climb above 2 yet. If you get a tiny little bit of reach, and you see the frequency kick above two or three, that generally shows that you have a minority hook. Or that you might be using a standard event or some messaging that just doesn't appeal to the larger quantity of those that you're targeting. Does that make sense? That's exactly what's happening. It's a 35 million audience. And every time we go $100, $200 spend on the first day, it's an 8x ROAS. By the time we hit... 500 to 600 dollars spent across four or five hooks with one creative it's plummeting to a 2x ROAS and we have to turn it off and then get our next UGC creator with the next five hooks and so because we didn't have the experience that you did we told the client that okay it's probably also the website being shit because it is a terrible funnel to be clear but then when speaking with you and also Nick, it was like, no, no, no, you need to just have better creative. No, you need to test more hooks. So as long as you're clear or you continue to believe. That, based on me describing this situation, it is the hooks— the creative and potentially the standard event— as you just said. Then we'll just continue to iterate that to get better and better, like I'm flying out to actually record a content with them in LA to be clear. But as long as you think that's the main problem, I'll continue pushing down that hole. If that makes sense. Yeah, everything I just said is very, very accurate and true. And. You solve the problem by hook iteration. By widening the messaging to appeal to a greater quantity of people. Sometimes even just trying out different standard events can help. Um, but yeah, try to have wider hooks. There's a great video inside of the Weekly Inner Circle group call library for you guys. a few pages in that is called Majority Hook Mastery. I would encourage that you watch that if you would like more information on that topic of how to create and craft and find the wider hooks. That video would be quite helpful to you prior to going and filming that in that example. Okay, looks like we have a few questions in the chat here. Nick asks, Nick, was that a question or you responded to someone else? I was asking you. Like... In the any example where you said, 'Oh, I'm like spending 50 grand in a day,' for example, and then each week I'm swapping shit out. Yeah. In that specific scenario. In that specific scenario, are you usually just creating new variations of the same angles? Because I imagine, like, there's still diminishing returns when you just create new variations of the same general message. Or are you also, or are you like swapping ad angles, like ad concepts in that week? Yeah, great question. So if it's like a time-based event, like, as an example, we were actually promoting an event just recently for the client Cody. She had that challenge funnel that we talked about and we were spending 30 to 50K a day there for a bit. And. Because it was a time-based event, I had the luxury of being able to use like time-sensitive hooks as well. So I would do broader messaging. And I would also do time-based messaging. Broader messaging just meant here's all the topics covered. Here's like some of the speakers. Here's like what you can expect out of the event. Here's like outcomes you can get out of the event. Those types of hooks. The time-based ones were generally image ads that I would pepper in there that would give or take like how much time was left. Or early bird pricing lifting, like things like that. And yeah, I had to swap those in and out every like three to five days max. I was able to just do a few tricks. To extend the life of some very well-performing creatives out of the whole batch of things we tested, very common things I've talked about prior but just to reiterate: adding b-roll to the standard videos we typically have clients shoot on multi-cam. So we'll have like a primary talking head shot and then like a secondary cam from a different angle. So we can create so many ads with that two-camera setup. We can create the standard talking head ad with captions. We can create the standard talking head ad with multiple angles with captions. We could do both of those iterations and swap in different B-roll footage. This creates a lot of variations that we can create, as you can imagine. We can. change the length that we allow one camera angle versus the other and how rapidly we switch between them to create more variations. Rather than just having to create and film brand new stuff. She had maybe filmed a total of 15 ads. In terms of unique hooks. We were able to produce a little over 175 creatives out of those 15 total video ads that she had originally shot. In addition to that, we probably rotated through about 40 to 50. image ads throughout about a month and a half, almost two months of promotion time. On having to swap everything out essentially every three to five days. Another instance is: You know, we're just spending that much, but it's not a time-sensitive event, meaning there's not like scarcity— I get to work in or. Ticket pricing increases, you know, it's just more of a We're just operating at that level of scale. So we need to get that spend off on a continuous basis. Simply put, when that occurs, it's the same logic. However, the client needs to sit down and film generally once a month. In some instances, like once every other month, if they're not as available. But, uh... Yeah, I mean, same logic. We're having the client generally film like 10 to 20 different ad hooks when we're operating at that scale of spend and just doing what we articulated to you there with. Ideally, the multi-cam, even if it's a single shot, we can still get a lot of life out of that, give or take the B-roll that we have access to. Does that make sense? Yeah. When you're doing it, like, let's say it's a call funnel instead of a time-sensitive funnel. Are you like, is the majority of your, are the majority of the ad hooks already proven concepts or the majority in that scenario, not proven concepts? Well, if we're just getting started, obviously they're not proven at all, you know, and we got to find what works and then start to scale it up. After we find something that works and we scale it up, the one thing that I noticed as a very common pattern amongst all of you is you operate with like extreme attachment to something that works and you attempt to use it like forever. Amen. We will like have the client refilm those same videos. We'll still take those same hooks and those same exact videos to be clear and do all the things I just said. To extend the life of them and make them new for when we need to overcome ad fatigue, but we'll also, every time that we like overcome fatigue and get the opportunity to test again, work in completely different hooks that might be similar to what has worked the best. You know, so a good example of this, like I use Cody Sanchez with you guys a lot as a client. So I'll use her again as an example here. I've talked about it in a video before with you guys where I've said. Okay, one of the number one hooks. It's like a majority hook is. 'Hey, baby boomers are dying and there's like 75 million of them and their businesses are either going to die with them or they need handed down. And like you could essentially. And this is where we vary the hook. OK, so it's like we can use that exact same first part and then just change what I'm about to describe as the second part. So imagine the second part of what I'm about to give as examples is the interchangeable part of the app. I could then follow that up with. There's 75 million of them and... Their businesses are going to die with them if you don't take it over. And they would love for you to come and take their business over so they have active income in their retirement. From you running their business and keeping it alive. And their kids don't want their business. And their kids aren't great at business, but you are. And they would love for you to come in and help them out so they could have some cash flow through their retirement and not have to let their business die. Um. So you can come in, grow their business more, exit from it. Give them a lump sum and a little bit of cash flow while you ran the business through their retirement and get a big lump sum yourself and play the game all over again. Like there's so many different. Like secondary parts of that ad. Being able to still use that like original main hook. You get what I mean? Yeah. And obviously, there's there's. specific second parts of that ad that work the best. But here's the thing. It's like when you're operating at that scale, ROAS is never a static number. That's the one big thing that most people get. Wrong. Like you'll have an audience type and a hook and set of messaging that might pair with that audience type. That gets let's just say a 10 to 1 row ass and you'll have another set of hooks with a different audience type that might get like a three to one row at. And you might think, okay, I'm going to bias all the spend towards the 10 to 1 ROAS. And less spend towards the three-to-one ROAS, we don't do that. We just scale every single thing that's working because ROAS is not a static number. ROAS is a variable number that can change and fluctuate. It is not a constant. It's essentially a variable that can flux. So long story short, yes, we'll absolutely try to scale up the thing that makes the most ROAS. But generally, what makes the most ROAS, like that, especially at scale, is a very niche hook to a very niche audience. The bigger the audience is and the bigger the hook is. That doesn't necessarily equal to. I'm going to make a fuckload of money off that. That's going to be my biggest ROAS campaign. It's generally the opposite. The more niche the hook and the more niche the messaging and the audience is, the bigger the ROAS, the wider the hook. And the wider the audience, the lower the ROAS is, but I can get infinitely more spend off on that type of combination compared to the performance. More scalable, more scalable. Exactly, yeah. Yeah, makes sense. Yeah, anyway, moral of the story is, is yeah, we don't generally have the luxury of... attachment, especially at scale. But we do have many tactics to like. You know, obviously continue to use the hook. Yeah, we'll mark more creatives rather than just focusing on a few creatives at scale. To exactly get it. Okay, cool. Um, Pierre says, 'Why does some business, like you talked about, never go over to cold? Is it just because they got used to warm audiences and cannot adjust? Yeah. Happens all the time, Pierre, mainly with... organic audiences and organic businesses. So if you have, let's use, let's use your guy Brez that you work with as a great example of this. So I would imagine that Brez's almost entire revenue stream comes from organic. And that he hasn't ventured into paid, especially not cold. Is that accurate or no? Yes, 100% accurate. Okay, and is there a sales team involved or is it all just direct to checkout? Servus, team. Okay. And so, how long do you think the sales team has had exposure to exclusively selling to those organic, very warmed-up audiences? Past six, seven, or eight months. Okay, now imagine. If they start paid advertising, and they introduce warm audiences. Remember, warm audiences could be just video viewers, subscribers, playlist viewers. Obviously, it could be all those social interactions, website visitors, all those things we talked about. But keep in mind, these people aren't just binging the organic content and taking the action on their own. You're officially like just placing an opportunity to come through the funnel in front of these people. So they're still warm. But, you know, they're not nearly as warm as what an organic lead is. Do you think the sales team will struggle with that adaptation specifically? 100%. Okay. And. the adaptation curve and how long a business is willing to endure that specific exposure comes down to the amount of money they're willing to lose while their team learns how to adjust. So it could take a team. you know, a day or two. It could take a team three months before they essentially become adapted to that audience type. okay? And keep in mind, those are people who are still aware of the business in that example, or in this case, the personal brand. Now, just expand that out another layer and say, okay, now you're going to get in front of people who they actively want to do. What your client teaches or what your business can help solve for them. Okay. They want to do it. They're an in-market demographic. They have no idea who you are. They've never consumed a piece of content from you. So you'd consider them on like a timeline of where they're at in the sales process. Your salespeople will articulate them with very bad feedback to just be not qualified. But generally, they're just early in the sales process. And the business hasn't dealt with that type of person yet. So the adaptation curve is longer. You have to spend more money. You're just literally burning money at the expense of the business adapting to be able to sell that audience type. Eventually, it obviously pays for itself many times over. But in the short term, you're literally just losing cash. You know, you then have to factor in the variables: are you seeing the things you want to see from your team along the way that make you confident they're going to adapt? You know, are you getting essentially exposure to them not going to adapt at all? And you got to get a whole totally new team. You know, there's. Simply put, a big challenge that presents itself. Especially when it involves sales teams and mainly and only when it involves sales teams. Totally different game when it involves just straight-up direct to checkout. Okay. But again, going from organic to warm. big hurdle in itself. Going from organic to warm to cold. Is like I don't know, trying to fucking free scale a building, you know, without a harness is kind of what it feels like perceptually to the business. It's just a big, scary challenge. Um, where they're just burning money, you know, so because an organic business, especially, is not even like necessarily perceptually spending money to acquire customers when they officially have to lose money to let their team adapt. To eventually acquiring customers profitably. And paying for those customers, you can see where I'm going with this, right? Yeah. So long story short, it's generally like, and it all starts obviously with the mindset of the business owner in that case. Having willingness to endure. You know, without that. The game ends there. From there, it's all about the sales team's ability to differentiate lead type and the sales process necessary. Typically, with colder audiences, they have to play more of an educator role, and there's more than a single call to close. If the sales team is, and no offense to younger guys, to be clear, but this generally happens with the younger closers. Younger closer is more so than anybody. fail to like differentiate lead tight. And as a result of that, they just give horrid feedback of like. hey, these people are too cold, you know? And as I mentioned, that's not the case. They're just too early in the sales process and they don't know how to deal with them. They don't know how to adapt to that educator role that we talk about here in the group. And the business obviously doesn't help supplement the closers. With any kind of content remarketing or anything on a marketing side that could help warm them up further. You know what I mean? So it's a... It's a challenge, simply put, that most businesses fail to overcome. That's all it comes down to. They're under-equipped and under-aware. Uh... of how to play the game. So they just. You know, they try to raw dog it and then fail and then just eventually capitulate to only doing whatever they consider themselves good at. You know what I mean? Yeah, makes sense. Okay. Um, Chris asks, add creatives in both the regular structure. Hydra and hammer them. Does this fatigue the creative faster? Would you advise again? Chris, can you unmute yourself and ask your question? I don't understand. Yeah, definitely. So, if we're running both the same creative, say it was just one video for sake of conversation, and you got it running both in Hydra and you have it in HammerThem as well, is that going to fatigue it faster? And would you pull it out of HammerThem? Or do you get up? Yeah, I think that's my question. No, that shouldn't make any impact because the Hydra is a direct response strategy. And once somebody converts on it, you'd obviously be excluding those people from seeing that ad again. And then I would just articulate that it doesn't make much sense to use a direct response ad in your hammer then because. To hammer them is content framing. And the Hammer Them is a very finite amount of time campaign. Meaning it targets somebody for a key window and then they should drop out of that said key window. So no, that should not cause any fatigue issues, but. The execution of that doesn't make much sense because the Hydra is a direct response strategy and the Hammer them is a content framing strategy. I would expect there to be like no instance where that would occur. Got it and just for context, in ours, I mean these videos, even though they're, they are contextual, I mean they're The freaking bangers. Say they look really good. They still address kind of like questions on the top, objections on the top, and they still double as pretty good direct response. And we're seeing a pretty good. uh response on them, so uh, you know, yeah, that makes sense. Just remind yourself that. You know, the hammer them. As soon as the person sees three seconds of that video, they won't see it again. So if they converted from it from an ad, they likely wouldn't get targeted by it anyway, based on how the hammer them strategy set up. Heard that. Yep. Makes sense. Appreciate that. OK, cool. OK, last. Last question and then we'll move on. Anybody have a final question on anything that we've already talked about to this point before we just start building out some audiences? Okay, cool. Just to be clear. Um, if you've noticed some of the things I've alluded to so far on this call. The very like underlying lesson to all of this. is There shouldn't be a disconnect. in who you target. and what type of messaging you target them with. So let me be clear when I say. You can. Absolutely. Unlock. A lot of different audience types that you previously might not have considered, just by starting the thought process of who to target. Based on in market, needs convinced, mass market. Warm audiences who are already aware of you. In addition to all of that, even just the messaging, can determine who we're probable to target. As an example of this, like if I go on Google AdWords to do some targeting. I can right away come up with a lot of creative ideas. Just based on 'Okay, I'm going to do YouTube ads.' And I'm going to target people who are already watching these channels. I think with that alone, you know, like, okay, I can now. I want to put a video in front of people who watch this type of content. Okay? Bye. I can think with the creative first. And then come up with the appropriate audiences to get that creative in front of. And vice versa. I can think with who I'm going to target. And then what creatives I can put in front of those people. So let's go in. To the ad accounts here. And. Let's just mess around with some campaigns. Just to build some audiences. First of all, on the ad set level, one thing I always want you guys to also think with is placement. Okay. So. Just as we were talking about, like, I can think with. the creatives first. I can also think with placement first. So, you know, as an example, I've sometimes gone in here. And I'll just run. Uh, like a real-time placement, or I'll just run like a story placement. You know, or I'll just run something in the feed. Keep this in mind as well for your creative output. Okay, so relative to what kind of... you know, account you have, you may or may not see different things both now and in the future. To how you go about doing your targeting. Let's just start with this. When you do... Warm audience targeting. Okay. Obviously, you go to the audiences section. You come here and you create an audience. These are all examples of what we could consider to be warm audiences. So we obviously have our websites and we can create 180-day audiences for all website traffic. We have, as an example, our email lists and our customer audiences. And, you know, sometimes we use these for our hammer them audiences as well. It's very important to note, like, Thinking with segmentation. Can also be very smart. For unlocking you know creative ideas like little niche opportunities. In the answer I provided to Nick, I think in the final part of what I was talking to him about. I'd mentioned like you can have really high ROAS campaigns. You're not going to get a lot of spend off on them in some instances, but... You know, if I segment. Small audiences that I can have a lot of context to. That can create a great opportunity for a high ROAS kind of niche campaign. And a lot of that starts here, just obviously in your audiences that you're creating. Okay, one of my favorites is videos. I will say, just keep in mind. The higher the threshold of watch time on a video, generally is indicative of you know, somebody who is a higher quality audience. But let me say this, okay? So... I'll use some of you guys on the call here as an example so you can understand it through self-awareness. If you watch any of my YouTube videos. Okay. Do you watch it? All the way through. Do you skip around in some of them? You know? And the question is: Is a video view audience built based on the amount of consumption of the video? Or is it based on the length that they reached in the video? To determine whether somebody watched 75% of it. So check this out. So if you notice here. People who have viewed at least 25% of your video. Okay. You might assume that. That means they watched from, you know, the start of the video to the first 25% of it. Whereas you got to be very mindful of the verbiage used here. Okay. 'Viewed' means a total consumption of at least 25% of the total video. It's not necessarily defined to be just to the first one-fourth of the video. Okay, so you got to be careful with some of these higher threshold audience types. I had a podcast that was an hour and 55 minutes long. And. With targeting that audience. I initially thought, okay, I want to target the people who watch. you know, the whole video. Okay. Like I would consider those people to be. some of the hottest out of everybody that I could target. Okay. When in reality, it's like that's that's not the case because the person who watches the longest duration of time in that video okay, is not necessarily the most qualified. Okay. So look, look at this here as an example. Okay. So this is a cold audience. Where I'm getting a uh— Am I tripping by saying that's a thousandth of a penny? Or is that? 100, does anybody know? Next. Is that a thousandth of a penny? 5 ,000... Am I articulating that right? I think it's 100. Yeah, I'm not. I honestly don't know. Because 0 . 01 would be one cent. Yeah. And then the 10th and the, yeah. Okay. Yeah. So, so this is what? Five hundredths of a, of a penny per. Whatever. So anyway. Look at this as an example here, okay? So. With The video view breakdown specifically. Um with a spend of $3 ,004. Okay. We have 4 ,777 people who got to a hundred percent. Uh... An hour and 55 minute video. Okay, 3,083 from cold, 201 from warm, and 1,493 from lookalikes, also cold. Okay. For three thousand dollars in spend, so if I take that three thousand and four dollars in spend, and I divide it by the 4,777 people. That watched, it cost me 62, technically 63 cents. To get somebody to watch 100% of a video. Okay. But isn't that weird? Like, really think about that. Have you noticed? Why are there more? Like, look at the quantity of people that watch 25% of an hour and 55 minute video. Is that not a great audience to still target? I mean, 25% of almost two hours is 30 minutes. You know, that's still a really long time. Now, when we go back to the audiences section and we're creating video view audiences, it's lumping all of the video views. With like, let's just say I select 25% and I just go and start selecting a bunch of videos. It's like... Does it make sense? To lump that all together? You know? I don't think so. I think it makes a lot more sense to have like a 25% audience for that one video. And then... Maybe I do like a 75% audience for all of like the 15-second videos, you know? So the length, you notice you can see here like the lengths, right? Like if I did 25% of six seconds. Would I want to include the 25% video that's fucking two hours long? You see what I mean? So when you're creating a video of your audiences, think about that, right? Because, like I said, like this is the hour and 55-minute video. 25% of that is obviously a far, far greater value. Compared to the other one. So keep that in mind when you're creating video view audiences and in most instances, you know, 25% of longer videos is fantastic. 25% of six seconds. I mean, yeah. Who fucking cares, right? It's like, I'd rather have 100% of six seconds. You see what I mean? So since there's such a wide range now of video lengths, that's why I bring that up. That alone can be monumental. For what type of person is added into your warm video view audiences, and obviously in here as well, you can segment further by having people who watch ad videos versus people who watch organic videos. So sometimes when creating warm audiences, and you just lump all of your warm audience videos together— from ad videos to the super short six-second videos to the fucking podcast. All of that combined, doesn't make a lot of sense. Think with segmentation when you're doing that, okay? You have your lead forms, which you can do for up to 90 days. And those 90-day audiences can be great, give or take the form. We have our Instagram and our Facebook accounts as well. These can be up to 365 days, and we have all these options. We absolutely want to create all of these. The only ones that you generally need to create on its own are the people who follow the account. Anyone who's a follower is generally good to put into its own audience for almost all these other ones, unless you're targeting the EU. Also, separate out messages. If you're targeting the EU. Okay, Facebook page, also great. I don't ever really see people having a lot of needs for the other ones. E-commerce, all my e-com guys, catalog and shopping is obviously great to also create audiences for. And anybody who runs any kind of events on Facebook. Keep all that in mind. So, okay, so long story short. Uh, back to building out some campaigns here. When I'm creating warm audiences, it's like I will just stack all of those together. However, okay, I'll also be thinking with. Hey, what can I do? To be able to target people. Who? Are potentially indifferent. Hapes. Of warm audience groups. Okay, so I might be targeting, like all of the Facebook, Instagram, website, visitors, email lists inside of one. And then maybe I add like the organic video views to that too. And then I could create a secondary warm audience that has all of like the ad video viewers. You see what I mean? Okay, so anyway. I just do giant stacks of all the warm audiences. A good example of this would be if I go into like just another account to show you guys some shit that's actually running. Um, so this is like a warm audience stack for a webinar campaign. Here's an example of how that looks. So it's like. I did all of the Instagram, all of Facebook. I separated out the followers and the likes. I did a web audience. And for the main pixel. And then I also did Instagram 50% viewers because almost all of the videos from this page are generally under a minute. And I want people who watch at least 30 seconds. Those are all the videos that I put inside of that specific video audience. Okay. You just do a big stack. Okay. Now. If we do some cold targeting. Okay, so cold targeting. We want... to be thinking with. Essentially, three main things, okay? These three main things are really simple to be thinking with. I like to do an interest stack. I like to do a broad targeting ad set, which is essentially just targeting nothing. And I also like to do lookalikes. Okay, you can get really creative with lookalikes when you're doing a test there. Lookalike testing, in my opinion, is very undermined for like the level of creativity you can have. I always try to keep my lookalike audiences to be defined to 1%. I don't like to go beyond 1% because keep in mind, lookalikes are built off data point similarities. To the initial uploaded audience, okay that's all it means— it's one percent of the country's population that has the data points that are most similar to the original uploaded audience. Okay. So when I do lookalikes, I want to think with high intent. Concentrated lists to upload. So my highest value customers is a great example of a lookalike audience. All my customers in general is obviously another great one. But in addition to that, like, let's say in your CRM, you marked people that showed up to calls. And you're doing a call funnel. A lookalike audience built on Qualified showed up calls is fucking awesome. Uh, I could, if I was doing an e-comm offer, as an example, when it was direct to checkout, I could do a lookalike audience based on people who reached. The add to cart standard event, when that fires off, I could do it based on people who had certain purchase order values. You see what I mean? And also based on the campaign's intention, like sometimes we'll run low ticket to high ticket offers. Sometimes we'll do webinars. Like if I do webinars, I'll create a lookalike audience based on people who showed up. I'll do lookalike audiences based on people who watched. to long thresholds into the webinar itself. I'll do lookalike audiences based on people who like booked calls or got to the checkout page of the webinar. You see what I'm saying? I want to do things that are generally far beyond just. the final outcome. I want to go a few steps back to what's considered a qualified action. Because those generally still work really well. Okay. To be clear, Nick asked a question like, why wouldn't you want to do... A 10% lookalike. Well, if you notice what I'm saying, Nick, like... I'm trying to do a lookalike stack. To increase my audience size with a bunch of different high-quality one percent audiences, does that make sense? So if I do the highest quality buyers— all the buyers, people who showed up. People who showed up and were qualified. People who watch to a certain threshold in a webinar. People who bought multiple upsells in the low-ticket to high-ticket ascension funnel. And I stack all those one percent audiences together, I still result in a higher quantity of audience size. Rather than having to just default to like one single audience type. Up to a 10% lookalike size. Because keep in mind, a lookalike is just... Finding people. who ideally match from a data point perspective. to the concentrated audience that you initially uploaded. to go find people similar to those. Again, if I do 10%, it's like, I'm pretty much just doing broad targeting, you know? And again, we'll do broad targeting in its own ad set anyway. Lookalikes I'm trying to use those customer lists I'm trying to use those concentrated quality lists to go find larger quantities of people. That's all I'm trying to do there. Same thing with interest stack targeting. I'm trying to be. And this is really important to note. Like when we do cold advertising nowadays, we're generally doing three main audiences: Interest Stacks, broad, and look-a-likes. Okay, so knowing that I'm already going to do broad targeting, I want my look-alikes to be concentrated. But sizable. I want my interest stacks to be concentrated but sizable. Broad is as broad as it gets. If I create too broad of an interest stack or too broad of a look-alike, I'm essentially just audience overlap at that point. And I have two competing ad sets that are both doing broad targeting. You know, I want to ideally create some separation between those three. That way there's not overlap. If there's too much overlap, you could argue that you really only need one ad set in that example for cold. And it would pretty much just be broad. You know, it's very important to consider when you're creating these kinds of things. So anyway. When we go and create some type of cold audience, okay, it's also important to note like this specific setup that you see here. This is the advantage audience targeting. Okay. And this essentially means that, like, what I'm providing to it, is broad. You know, that's pretty much it. It's pretty much as broad as it gets. When I'm at this stage of advertising and I see this specific setup. So everything that I add into it— as like targeting options— is at the end of the day, really, really just a suggestion. You know, it's not necessarily like I'm even getting the opportunity to do a lot of targeting. Okay. So keep that in mind, like very important consideration. That on this platform specifically, they're just pushing you at all times to do broad targeting. Okay. That's what they fucking love now on this platform. And as much as. Some advertisers hate it. Because you don't have as much control. It can be really good, but it's a whole different set of lessons. A lot of those lessons come down to. OSS. Targeting people. With proper messaging that increases our odds of having. you know, better people reached in our campaigns. When we do broad targeting, if we are not specific with our messaging, then long story short, you know, you're going to have a bad time because you're going to reach a whole bunch of people with a very diffused. defeat, like you're just not going to reach good people if you don't align your messaging. And again, you'll know that you have it if you have this stupid. Advantage, whatever shit turned on over here. So anyway, keep that in mind. Okay, when I do... Cold targeting, like, let me give you an example of, you know, a cold audience inside of this account that we're running to. So this is a good example of what we call the wealth stack. So, when we're targeting rich people specifically, like, first of all, just look at this audience size over here on the right. You know, it's 98 million to 116 million people, big, okay? But that's not just based on all of these different targeting options that I've selected. It's because the advantage detailed targeting has been turned on on this account. So again, this essentially means I'm giving it suggestions with who I want it to target. Who I feed back to the pixel is going to determine who it goes after. The messaging that I have on my ads, on my landing pages, and all my video assets associated with those two variables is going to dictate who it goes after. But it's still good to give it suggestions. Okay, although yes, you could technically argue that this is still broad targeting. It does still leverage your targeting suggestions here and tries to initially bias towards those people. But as described, it will go after whoever the fuck it wants. When you see that little option there. When I do interest stack targeting, this is a great example of how I typically do it. I see advertisers make a very common mistake of like breaking out. these individual interests into a bunch of different ad sets. Keep in mind that's an awful idea. It goes against all the best practices that the platform wants you to do. The platform wants you to have as simplified of structures as you can. especially nowadays with how it's all set up. If I have detailed targeting turned on or that advantage audience targeting turned on, it's like all I'm really getting is a bunch of different ad sets that are essentially broad targeting that are going to overlap each other. So that's not good. Like, we do not want to do that. We want to create as simple of a structure as we can. So again, every interest that you would want to target, just throw it into one interest stack. is generally the best practice that reduces inflation, increases longevity, and gives you the control that you're after. It is important to note: if you separate it, the intention for why you're trying to separate it is usually because you're trying to see which interest is technically working better. But just remember, if you see this— Or... This. Okay. You're not. Targeting these things exclusively, even if you break them all out. Okay. You're technically just targeting. Broad audiences in almost every ad set. Okay. Does that make sense to everybody? You know, it's a little confusing comparatively to how it used to be where all your targeting actually mattered. So therefore you could argue that, you know, separating everything out would give you the visibility. But now, like I said, you're just creating a bunch of competing ad sets and a bunch of overlap for yourself, which is ultimately going to fuck you. So anyway. One interest stack. Yeah. One inch of stack. One lookalike stack. And then one ad set that just does broad targeting. Broad targeting just means like we're literally not going to target anything. We might define the age to be fair and like the geographic location, sometimes the gender, but like we're not doing any of this. Okay, cool. So. Anyway, some accounts you have. Like you'll just do cold targeting, okay? So this is a good example. Like this account in particular, I only have $1,100 a day to spend. And this was a brand new account when I initially started it. So for this account specifically, like, well, why not start with just broad? Why not start with just, you know, lookalikes? Well, I didn't have any lookalike data to use, first of all. Second of all, because it was a new account and a new pixel, and it didn't have any data to leverage. I wanted to try to influence it as much as possible towards the ideal demographic that I wanted to have convert. OK, so an interest stack is what I chose to start with here. And that's played out extremely well. This is just this month's data: a cost per qualified call of 105. And that's been about the case in all these campaigns that have run so far for this. We've had two different campaigns so far. You can see this is the total quantity of qualified calls and the cost differences between the two of them. We've gotten exceptional feedback from the closers on the sophistication of these leads and how qualified they are. And that's a good example of you know, kind of a best practice to use when you're building out audiences in a new account with a new offer. When you don't really have anything else to leverage yet. You know, you don't have lookalike data. Broad targeting doesn't make as much sense because you don't have a conditioned pixel. It's like in those cases, I'm always starting with. An interest stack and again, this offer specifically targets rich people. This is an offer that requires people to have $70,000 up front. Every single ad starts off with, you know, if you have $70,000 in capital. This is a dynamic creative ad here. And again, optimized for a cost per qualified call in this case. So now that I have— you know, 342 qualified calls that are validated. I have what I'd consider to be a great cost per call, given the price of this product. And I also have about 37K in spend. Once I get— I'd say about 500 people would be ideal, just factoring in match rate. I will build out some of those lookalike audiences I talked about. So I'll export all of the buyers, which probably won't exceed 100 yet. So I really won't get to use that right away in my example. I'll export all of the people who are qualified. Okay, that were marked as qualified. I'll create a lookalike from that. I'll also. export all the people who showed up. I'll use that. And in addition to that, I'll just use all the people who booked a call here. Okay, and I'll create three lookalikes off that. And I'll test that out. For now. I'm limited in what I'm doing. I only have two closers on this account. Those closers are currently maxed out. We are one month and one week in on this offer. So long story short. I'm most likely going to introduce lookalikes and broads. Probably in about another month. You know, we made a little over 100K in the first 30 days on this offer, just getting it launched and off the ground. There's about 300K in the pipeline here. Seems like we've got about a two-week deal cycle currently. So I'm going to let all those buyers accumulate. I'm going to let more revenue accumulate before I take on risks that aren't as probable to convert. So anyway. As you can see, just across multiple accounts, there's like very common practices. This is for a webinar. So this is a good example here. These two campaigns, I'm working alongside of another advertiser in this account. So not all of this is my data, just these two campaigns at the top here are my data. When I came in, I lowered the cost per webinar registration by about $3 to $5, give or take the campaign and the time of it. Uh, let's just set this out to like the last— I think I came in sometime in March. I don't remember the exact day. So we'll just set it to that. So you can see. Like this is an example of one of their historical webinar campaigns that's still running right now. These are an example of these two. So in this particular case, this advertiser has never run warm audiences before. Only run cold. Okay. So just one of the easiest things to initially do is do exactly what I described with the warm audience stack. So an Instagram audience, a Facebook audience. This specific creator, they didn't do a lot organically. So I just used 25% of ad videos. What that specific audience represents, and then all the website visitors over the last 180 days. That's the warm audience stack in this account, which again, has never historically been targeted. And then just to be clear, this is also a real estate offer. So I did that same exact interest stack that we just saw in that other account doing calls I just described. Working really well, about the same audience size as this one here. Being a little bit greater, it looks like. So again. Differences here. Just one cold, one warm. Um, this account does have a lot of data, okay? On people who have opted in for webinars, okay? Now, here's the thing. I was given a budget of $1,500 a day to start. So. The one thing that you'll notice I like to bias towards when I'm doing cold targeting first. is interest stats. Okay. So out of the three options for audience types that I could target when I'm doing cold. I'm not going to go broad first. I'm not going to use lookalikes first. I'm generally going to do a cold interest stack, especially just given my... length of time I've been advertising and how good I am at targeting rich people. I have a really good interest stack that I've shared with you guys many times. For cold targeting rich people. Okay. So I knew I could come in, get very qualified people through the door right away. And that was what I chose to do in that example with that interest stack. Now, here's the thing. There's a new sales team that's going to start, I believe, this coming Wednesday. Now, that's really good because the current sales team are pieces of shit. Without naming who it is, I'm sure those of you who have been in here a while know who it is. They are getting replaced. And we're putting a new sales team into the account. So, due to the budget constraint, okay, of $1,500 a day. Due to the fact that they had never targeted warm audiences before. And just due to the fact I have a nice proven interest stack that I know has the highest probability to work. I'm taking the budget. I'm splitting it right down the middle in that example. 750 to warm, 750 to that cold interest stack. Now, once that new sales team settles in, and once the ROI is officially validated from the new sales team, and I get unlimited budget to just scale into right away, literally right away upon, hey, you can start scaling. I'll dupe out that cold campaign. I'll add a lookalike audience. I'll add a broad audience. I'll obviously retain the interest stack. And I'll just start scaling the shit out of it. I'll let them all compete with each other. I'll probably switch it to ABO instead of CBO to really push spend on all three of those cold audience types. um control them manually for which ones I flux up and flux down and just push it to the limit. But again, since I'm limited on $1 ,500 in budget, I'm not going to do that. I'm going to bias towards what I know has a higher probability to work initially. Okay, cool.
