Does anybody else have questions on what we talked about there? The main thing I want you to understand is. The width of that audience in almost all instances, especially on Facebook and Instagram, is very wide nowadays. But that's okay. It's not going after that quantity of people. It never will. That number is an illusion. I'll never reach 70 to 80 or 100 million people. I am probable to reach the amount of people that are going to convert or probable to convert. On the standard event and messaging combination that I have. That could be tens of thousands of people before I start to see the frequency climb. So back to this example. It's like if I incorporate frequency as a statistic into this account, you can see here I've reached 146,000 people. I only have a frequency of 1. 43. From a data-driven perspective, that means that I'm probable to still reach far more people. OK, before if I if I saw my frequency climb. That essentially means I'm out of people. Okay, with that specific hook. And with that specific messaging. With that specific standard event, okay? So I could either change the standard event in a follow-up test campaign. I could change out my... creative and just use wider hooks. Okay, if I use this one as an example, again, these are both cold audiences. You'll see here, okay, I reached 157,000 people and then my frequency climbed above two. That campaign was killed. This is the relaunched version of the campaign up here at the top. That has a reach of 122,000. The frequency is starting to climb again. So to be clear. If I saw that around 157,000 people, just like that first ad set. That my frequency climbs above two. Then I would know. Okay. I have a limit of about 150,000 people. That are probable to convert on the current messaging that I'm using those hooks and my standard event combination. So like I said, I could just change up the hooks. I could try to be a little broader in my appeal. I could also change my standard event, okay? And those would give me possibilities to reach beyond that. But you'll see here, it's like, I have a 65 million to 76 million audience. So that's an illusion, okay? This is always an illusion. If it's narrower, meaning if this is smaller, Just remember. If I'm only reaching 150,000 people out of 60 million before my frequency starts to climb, what would that indicate if I only had a million-person audience? What would that indicate if I only had 100,000-person audience? That would mean my frequency is probable to climb far sooner with far less people, no matter the hooks I use. So the broader the audience. Again, the more people you're probable to reach, okay? Budget can also determine this, by the way. If I just relaunch this campaign and instead of spending $1,000 a day, if I relaunched it with... you know, fucking $20,000 a day. It's like, I am more probable to reach people beyond that. Okay, for sure I am. However, you know, you got to work within your budget constraints. If budget increases aren't just an overnight decision for you to like 20X your budget to reach 20X more people. you know, like I said, standard event tests and just hook tests can be a great way to reach additional people beyond who you're kind of already stuck reaching. And this is not a small group of people. I mean, there's still a lot of people to reach. Anyway, just keep that in mind in your targeting as well. Same lesson applies to warm audiences. Okay. Again, sorry about that. I interrupted somebody. Does anybody have questions on that? Anything I've talked about here so far? Just on the, you know, when you said you'd dupe out the cold audience, you'd dupe out the campaign, sorry, and then you'd add an ad set for remarketing and you'd add one ad set for interest. Now we've got three ad sets, right? On ABO instead of CBO. And like, let's say, out of those three ad sets, two of them start to get ad fatigue, but the one of the ad sets continues to perform. Then we just turn off those two ad sets and we keep scaling that one ad set until that fatigues and then we'll dupe out again. Is that, have I understood that correctly? Yes, that's definitely one way to do it. There are many ways to go about doing what you just described. So, the way that you articulated it, just to reiterate it. If I have that campaign and it's got cold interests, broad and lookalikes, and one of three or two of three fatigue, and I technically could relaunch them and they're profitable. I might just break them out into their own campaign, you know, so I might end up with three campaigns. One for cold interest stack, one for broad, and one for lookalike. Which I actually have an account right now set up on my laptop that I don't have on my main desktop here. I access it through another account. That's why. And long story short. In that specific account, I have that exact structure I just articulated. I have one campaign that has a single ad set in it for broad, one campaign that has a single ad set for lookalikes, and one campaign that has a single ad set for my interest stack. And it's for that exact reason. There's like variable fatigue between the three of them. And as a result of that, I broke them out on their own. That way, when one fatigues, given that all three are profitable, I can just relaunch it with a new ad creative right away. I don't have to wait. If I do it in the way that you described. And again, there's nothing wrong with doing it that way. That would also still work. I would kill the fatigued ones and just wait until that other lasting one fatigues. And then I'd relaunch the campaign, let all three start over again. And, you know, go from there. Jeremy, I'm noticing that in the campaigns, I'm receiving a message that I've never received before. When I shut down a campaign, it suggests that I reallocate the budget from that campaign into another. Do you know if they updated their algo? Because your traditional advice was like, 'Hey, if you're going to hyperscale, right, we're taking 1 ,000, 60 ,000, let's say.' It's you essentially duplicate that campaign, start it at that new hyperscale budget, and then launch. Do you know if they updated the algo where you can just reallocate a piece of a campaign to another? So let's say we have two campaigns running at $1,000. I shut down one and says, 'Hey, you want to push this $1,000 over to the other?' Would that still screw shit up? Dope. When you go to the casino, does the casino want you to continue spending money? In the instance that you stop spending money on one game or, you know, one slot machine, what do they want you to do? Spare more money. Right. Yeah. So messages like that, like I don't give a fuck about, you know, it's like I don't have to move the budget over to another campaign right away. If I want to, I can, but it's like. I mean, you're talking to the casino. You know, it's like. You got to be mindful of the fact that the casino obviously wants you to continue to spend their money. And, uh... You know, if you forget that you're in the casino, like you're going to get taken advantage of quite frequently. It's like the stupid, uh... audience advantage, the advantage audience shit. It's like, it's just what it is. Just imagine your Facebook, right? You got a whole quantity of fucking plebs on planet earth that never get targeted by fuck all. And therefore you have a large swath of people that like are getting no ads or very little ads or that inventory of people that could be served ads is just underutilized. What would you do? You would force broad targeting onto everybody and then masquerade it as a great idea to enable people, you know, oh, we're going to help businesses by enabling them to reach people beyond who they think they need to target. It's like, no, dude, that's a fucking casino move. To help them make more money on people that they historically aren't getting a high quantity of advertisers targeting. You know, it's like. It's a simple casino tactic. When you get a fucking message when you turn off a campaign, it's like, 'Oh, are you sure you don't want to reallocate the budget?' It's like. Dog, of course they fucking want you to reallocate the budget. They got continued quarter over quarter growth to maintain. Otherwise, their stock's going to lose billions of fucking dollars, you know? It's like the casino, dude, you know, no. And have awareness about, you know, what you're operating within. Otherwise, you get taken advantage of. They don't like, yes. They have your best interest in the sense that, unlike a casino, a casino literally wants to take all your money. And they don't want you to make money in Facebook's casino or any other ad platforms' casino. They do want you to make money because you'll come back and continue giving them more money. It's more of a win-win scenario on an ad platform because, obviously, if you fail to make back money, you'll not spend anything. If you make more money, you'll give them more money. So. They have your best interest. So there are plenty of things that are good ideas to take action on. But... almost everything that they recommend or do, for the most part, is for their own self-interest and their stock value. You know? But the platform as a whole is still beneficial to use and leverage because, like I said, it's set up to be a win-win. But no, messaging like that is. I don't give a fuck about messaging like that. I just remind myself I'm in the casino. Okay. Appreciate it. Other questions? To confirm what you were explaining at the beginning, The reason why we want to use 1% lookalikes is because that's the most concentrated audience to the source that we are providing. They have the most data points similar to those that you initially uploaded as, like, the concentrated list you're creating the lookalike from. Yes. Is there any scenario in which we will go to a 10%? I have literally not a single time in my career gotten a beneficial result from doing that. Got it. I mean, to be fair, like I said, you're just doing broad targeting when you do that. So you're literally doing that by just doing broad targeting anyway. Ten percent. of a country's population. is Like I said, essentially the equivalent of just doing broad targeting. So there's no difference between that and broad. So it's like. I've done broad many times and I've gotten great results many times with broad, but No, I mean. I'm not doing that through 10% lookalike. Thank you. Okay, other questions? Okay, cool.
